hallucinations.description: The paper claims the model uses a Neural Functional Transformer via the nfn library with multi-head attention and permutation-equivariant NFT attention. The provided code instead implements a simplified MLP-style backbone (`nn.Sequential` of linear/ReLU layers) and explicitly states it is a simplification rather than the nfn/NFT implementation. Therefore the methodology as described in the paper does not match the actual implementation.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "The tokenized sequences are processed by a Neural Functional Transformer using the nfn library implementation (Zhou et al., 2023). The configuration uses 4 attention layers, 8 attention heads, hidden dimension 256, and dropout 0.1. NFT attention provides permutation-equivariance..."
Code (`cawe/models/cawe.py`): "# Simplified NFT backbone (instead of using nfn library for PoC) # Using standard transformer-style attention as permutation-equivariant approximation" followed by `self.nft_backbone = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(token_dim, nft_channels), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(nft_channels, token_dim), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(token_dim, token_dim))`

hallucinations.description: The paper describes architecture-specific tokenizers that flatten convolution kernels, Q/K/V attention matrices, and fully connected weight matrices, projecting them into a shared token space. The actual tokenizer implementation is much cruder: it slices or pads flattened tensors to length 512 and, for MLPs, even accepts any 2D `weight` tensor regardless of whether it is an FC layer. This is a materially different method from the one reported.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "For each Vision Transformer layer, Query, Key, and Value weight matrices are extracted from attention heads, flattened, and projected to dimension 128." and "For each fully-connected layer with weights W ∈ R^(n_out × n_in), the weight matrix is flattened and projected to dimension 128."
Code (`cawe/tokenizers/tokenizers_simple.py`): `if ('attn' in key.lower() or 'attention' in key.lower()) and 'weight' in key and param.dim() == 2: flattened = param.flatten(); if flattened.shape[0] >= 512: layer_repr = flattened[:512] else: layer_repr = torch.nn.functional.pad(flattened, (0, 512 - flattened.shape[0]))`
For MLP tokenizer: `if ('fc' in key.lower() or 'linear' in key.lower() or 'weight' in key) and param.dim() == 2:`

hallucinations.description: The paper reports a specific overall result for the heterogeneous test set, ρ=0.294 with 95% CI [-0.056, 0.586] on n=30. The executable experiment logs instead show substantially different measured results on a 60-sample test set, including ρ=0.5994 and ρ=0.7028 with CI [0.5242, 0.8211]. This indicates the paper's reported main experimental outcome is not the result shown by the provided code execution.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "Overall performance on the full heterogeneous test set was ρ=0.294 (95% CI: -0.056 to 0.586)." and "On the full heterogeneous test set (30 samples, 10 per architecture family), CAWE achieved overall Spearman ρ=0.294..."
Code logs (`experiment_run.log`): "Test samples: 60" and "Test Spearman ρ: 0.7028" and later "95% CI: [0.5242, 0.8211]"
Also `experiment.log`: "Test Spearman ρ: 0.5994"

hallucinations.description: The paper claims all five validation components passed their prespecified thresholds and gives exact values for per-family ablations, silhouette score, baseline deltas, p-values, and robustness. The code for the integrated validation does not actually compute all of these values: Component 4 is explicitly skipped and assumed to pass, Component 5 is simplified to a single check on base CAWE rho, and the saved `mechanism_validation_results.json` contains hard-coded looking summary numbers inconsistent with the actual implementation and logs. Thus the detailed component results in the paper are not supported by executed experiments.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "All five validation components passed their prespecified thresholds" with table entries including "Random forest | Δρ (p-value) | 0.12 (0.008)" and robustness values across D ∈ {64,128,256,512}.
Code (`run_mechanism_validation.py`): "# Simplified: Report NA (would require feature engineering) component4_pass = True  # Assume pass for now" and "# Simplified: Just report original CAWE performance component5_pass = cawe_rho > 0.65".
This directly contradicts the paper's claim that random forest and robustness experiments were actually run as reported.

hallucinations.description: The paper states generalization gaps were computed as test accuracy minus train accuracy evaluated on CIFAR-10 for the zoo models. The logs show that when ImageNet is unavailable, the code falls back to 'literature-reported gaps' or 'noise-based gap estimation', meaning the target values were not actually computed from CIFAR-10 evaluation as claimed.
hallucinations.evidence:
Paper: "Generalization gaps were computed as (test accuracy - train accuracy) evaluated on CIFAR-10."
Logs (`experiment.log`): repeated warnings "ImageNet dataset not found at ./data/imagenet. Using literature-reported gaps."
Logs (`experiment_run_fixed.log`): repeated warnings "ImageNet dataset not found at ./data/imagenet. Using noise-based gap estimation."

hallucinations.description: Several cited works appear misattributed or likely nonexistent in the exact form given. In particular, the reference "Falk et al. (2025). The Vision Transformer Model Zoo. In ICLR 2025." is presented as a formal publication, but no corroborating evidence is provided in the code or materials, and it is highly likely to be an invented citation. The other Zhou references also appear suspiciously inconsistent with the described contributions, but this Falk citation is the clearest likely fabrication.
hallucinations.evidence: References section: "Falk, T., Mai, F., Baum, A., Fernandez, D. M., Cremers, D., and Akata, Z. (2025). The Vision Transformer Model Zoo. In ICLR 2025."

overall_assessment: Hallucinations were found. The most serious issues are methodology mismatches and unsupported/fabricated experimental claims: the paper describes a true NFT/nfn-based attention model and fully executed 5-component validation, while the code implements a simplified MLP-style surrogate and skips or assumes key experiments. The reported headline metrics also do not match the provided run logs.
